coredata.dc:title: Aptamer-functionalized Fe3O4@MOF nanocarrier for targeted drug delivery and fluorescence imaging of the triple-negative MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells
coredata.dc:creator: Alijani, Hamed
Noori, Abolhassan
Faridi, Nassim
Bathaie, S.Zahra
Mousavi, Mir F.
coredata.dc:description: The development of efficient drug delivery and imaging approaches is of great importance for both diagnostic and therapeutic purposes. This is especially important for the triple-negative breast cancers for which chemotherapy is often the mainstay of treatment. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have recently attracted tremendous attention as nanocarriers that can be loaded with a wide variety of therapeutic as well as bioimaging agents. Herein, the Fe3O4 core UiO-66-NH2 MOF shell nanostructures, Fe3O4@MOF, were loaded with the anticancer drug doxorubicin (DOX), and the loaded nanostructures were conjugated to highly fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) and then capped with the nucleolin-binding aptamer, AS1411. The Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt nanocarriers were specifically unlocked in the presence of the nucleolin-overexpressing cancer cells, resulted in the release of the loads. The drug delivery process rendered more efficient by pH-dependent controlled release kinetics that provides an enhanced therapeutic outcome. The stimuli-responsive drug delivery system reveals a rather selective permeation of the Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt nanocarriers into the triple-negative MDA-MB-231 human breast cancer cells, compared to the normal HUVEC cells. Cytotoxicity experiments revealed that the nanocarriers inhibit cell proliferation and induce selective apoptosis towards the MDA-MB-231 cancer cells (>77% at 24 ​h); whereas, less than 10% apoptosis was induced in the normal HUVEC cells. In addition, we encapsulated CDs into the nanocarriers, thereby, the nanocarriers exhibit fluorescence imaging functionality, thus can effectively contribute to monitoring biodistribution of the nanocarriers in vitro. Based on these findings, the Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt nanocarriers can serve as a promising multifunctional material for cancer therapy and cellular bioimaging.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the Fe3O4@MOF core-shell nanostructures
Preparation of DOX-loaded and CDs-labeled Fe3O4@MOF
Conjugation of the aptamer to Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: For the preparation of the Fe3O4@MOF core-shell nanostructures, 25 ​mg of Fe3O4 was added to 10 ​mL of DMF and ultrasonicated for 30 ​min (preparation of Fe3O4 is described in the Supporting Information, SI). Then, the obtained dispersion was added to 8 ​mL of DMF solution containing precursors of the UiO-66-NH2 MOF (37.5 ​mg of ZrCl4, and 29 ​mg of NH2-BDC). The resulting mixture was placed in a preheated oven at 80 ​°C for 12 ​h and then held at 100 ​°C for 24 ​h. After cooling down to room temperature, the resulting pale brown solid was magnetically separated and washed several times with ethanol and DI water. The purified Fe3O4@MOF core-shell nanostructures were dispersed in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) and stored at 4 ​°C for subsequent use.Supporting Information. The Supporting Information is available free of charge at DOI: XXXX. The Supporting Information associated with this paper contains syntheses of UiO-66-NH2 nanocrystals and carbon dots, drug loading and release, cell culture, cytotoxicity assay, and physico-chemical characterization of the materials (FE-SEM, EDS, FT-IR, UV–vis, thermal analysis, magnetization studies, and BET and BJH analyses).This work was made possible through financial support from the Tarbiat Modares University Research Council as well as the Research Core of Tarbiat Modares University, grant number IG-39802 (M.F.M.).

This work was made possible through financial support from the Tarbiat Modares University Research Council as well as the Research Core of Tarbiat Modares University , grant number IG- 39802 (M.F.M.).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Electron microscopy characterizations. (a) FE-SEM image of the Fe3O4@MOF core-shell nanostructures. (b) HR-TEM images of the Fe3O4@MOF nanostructures. The inset shows the SAED pattern of the Fe3O4@MOF nanostructures. (c) HR-TEM images of carbon dots. The insets show the high magnification image of a single particle (top) and the SAED pattern of the carbon dots (bottom).

Spectroscopic characterizations. (A) FT-IR spectra of (a) carbon dots, (b) AS1411 aptamer, (c) Fe3O4@MOF, (d) Fe3O4@MOF-CDs, (e) Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs, and (f) Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt. (B) XRD patterns of (a) Fe3O4, (b) MOF, (c) Fe3O4@MOF, (d) pure carbon dots, and (e) Fe3O4@MOF-CDs. (C) The UV–vis absorption spectra of the as-prepared AS1411 aptamer solution (red) and the supernatant of the aptamer solution after being conjugated to the Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs nanostructures (blue). (D) the PL excitation (red) and emission spectra (blue) of carbon dots. The inset shows photographs of the carbon dots suspensions under daylight (I) and UV light (II). (E) The PL spectra of the carbon dots excited at different excitation wavelengths. The inset shows the corresponding normalized PL emission.

Drug loading characterization. (A) Variation of the DOX loading capacity of the Fe3O4@MOF nanostructures as a function of the DOX concentration. (B) UV–vis spectra of free DOX at different concentrations. (C) UV–vis spectra of the DOX solutions after incubation with the Fe3O4@MOF nanostructures, (after removal of the nanocarriers with an external magnet). (D) FT-IR spectra of DOX (magenta), Fe3O4@MOF (blue), and Fe3O4@MOF-DOX (green). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Cytotoxicity studies of normal and cancer cells. (A) Cell viability of HUVEC cells incubated for 24 ​h with different concentrations of (a) Fe3O4@MOF, (b) Fe3O4@MOF-DOX, (c) Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs, and (d) Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt. (B) Cell viability of MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells incubated for 24 ​h with different concentrations of (a) Fe3O4@MOF, (b) Fe3O4@MOF-DOX, (c) Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs, and (d) Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt. (C) Flow cytometry plots of MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells after 24 ​h treatment with (a) control, (b) Fe3O4@MOF-DOX, (c) Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs and (d) Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt.

Cell imaging and monitoring the drug delivery process. Fluorescence imaging of the live MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells treated with Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-Apt and Fe3O4@MOF-CDs-Apt for 6 ​h. Note that the images are obtained using special filter sets to allow easy visual interpretation of the treatments.

In vitro drug release profiles at two different pH’s. In vitro DOX release from the Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt nanocarriers at different time intervals at pH 5.5 (blue) and 7.4 (magenta). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Schematic illustration of the synthetic procedure of the nanocarrier, and targeted drug delivery. (Top) Schematic illustration of the synthesis of UiO-66-NH2 MOF on the as-synthesized magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles, and post-modification of the nanostructure with DOX loading, carbon dots encapsulation, and nucleolin-binding aptamer (AS1411) conjugation. (Bottom) Schematic illustration of the uptake and internalization of the Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt nanocarriers into the nucleolin-overexpressing triple-negative MDA-MB-231 human breast cancer cells via a nucleolin-mediated interaction and pH-triggered DOX release in lysosome or endosome of the cancer cells.
Comparison of the MOF-based nanocarriers for cancer treatment.

head.ce:title: Aptamer-functionalized Fe3O4@MOF nanocarrier for targeted drug delivery and fluorescence imaging of the triple-negative MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of efficient drug delivery and imaging approaches is of great importance for both diagnostic and therapeutic purposes. This is especially important for the triple-negative breast cancers for which chemotherapy is often the mainstay of treatment. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have recently attracted tremendous attention as nanocarriers that can be loaded with a wide variety of therapeutic as well as bioimaging agents. Herein, the Fe3O4 core UiO-66-NH2 MOF shell nanostructures, Fe3O4@MOF, were loaded with the anticancer drug doxorubicin (DOX), and the loaded nanostructures were conjugated to highly fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) and then capped with the nucleolin-binding aptamer, AS1411. The Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt nanocarriers were specifically unlocked in the presence of the nucleolin-overexpressing cancer cells, resulted in the release of the loads. The drug delivery process rendered more efficient by pH-dependent controlled release kinetics that provides an enhanced therapeutic outcome. The stimuli-responsive drug delivery system reveals a rather selective permeation of the Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt nanocarriers into the triple-negative MDA-MB-231 human breast cancer cells, compared to the normal HUVEC cells. Cytotoxicity experiments revealed that the nanocarriers inhibit cell proliferation and induce selective apoptosis towards the MDA-MB-231 cancer cells (>77% at 24 ​h); whereas, less than 10% apoptosis was induced in the normal HUVEC cells. In addition, we encapsulated CDs into the nanocarriers, thereby, the nanocarriers exhibit fluorescence imaging functionality, thus can effectively contribute to monitoring biodistribution of the nanocarriers in vitro. Based on these findings, the Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt nanocarriers can serve as a promising multifunctional material for cancer therapy and cellular bioimaging.

We prepared Fe3O4@UiO-66-NH2 MOF shell nanocarriers. The nanocarriers were loaded with DOX as well as fluorescent carbon dots and were capped with the nucleolin aptamer. The multi-stimuli-responsive nanocarriers demonstrated excellent cytotoxicity to the triple-negative MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells cancer cell, and were used for cell imaging as well.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: With about 17.0 million new cases each year and 9.5 million deaths, cancers are a complex set of diseases and among the leading causes of death worldwide [1]. Chemotherapeutics have consistently increased cancer survival rates over the past decade. However, the chemotherapeutic agents are nonselective, damage normal cells and tissues in addition to the cancerous one, and causing severe undesirable side effects [2]. In addition, because of the relatively poor bio-accessibility of the chemotherapeutic drugs to cancerous tissues, higher doses of the drugs are required, leading to an increased risk of toxicity against normal cells. Furthermore, most anticancer drugs, including doxorubicin (DOX) as the most commonly used drug for the treatment of cancers, show a narrow therapeutic window, that is the range of doses at which a chemotherapeutic agent is effective. However, it is usually accompanying with some known adverse effects. The adverse reactions of free DOX are nausea, vomiting, hair loss, and suppression of the bone marrow [3,4]. While DOX is an indispensable core drug for the treatment of breast cancer, its long-term utilization not only causes cancer cell resistance to chemotherapy, but also triggers some serious adverse effects including life-threatening heart and brain damages [5–7]. It has also been proven that the DOX-triggered hepatic, renal, and cardiotoxicity is being induced by massive production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and reactive nitrogen species (RNS) [8,9]. High levels of ROS and RNS may activate cytotoxic signals leading to DNA damage, attenuation in protein synthesis and etc. [10]. Therefore, it is indispensable to develop novel therapeutic approaches that can specifically target cancerous cells, thereby reducing unintended side effects while improving treatment efficacy. During the past decade, a series of new technologies and versatile materials [2] including polymer-based nanoparticles [11–14], liposomal and lipid-based drug delivery systems [15–19], quantum dots and inorganic nanoparticles [20–23], peptide- and protein-based nanocarriers [24–30], as well as micelles [31–33] and dendrimers [34–37] have been developed to facilitate the delivery of chemotherapeutics to tumor sites with improved therapeutic efficacy. Although several nanoparticle-based chemotherapeutics either are clinically approved or are in various stages of clinical/preclinical development [2], there is still a tremendous need to develop novel drug delivery systems with better targeting ability and suppressed adverse side effects.

Attaching aptamers as tumor recognition elements has been proven beneficial for targeted delivery of chemotherapeutic agents to tumor cells [38–44]. Aptamers, as single-stranded DNA or RNA oligonucleotides that bind to their targets with high affinity and specificity, possess several advantages over their counterpart antibodies including long-term stability, flexibility to be modified, higher binding affinity, pH stability, and limited immunogenicity [45–47]. However, the utilization of aptamer-functionalized nanocarriers for drug delivery applications generally requires a trade-off among payload capacity, serum stability, and cell internalization efficacy. To remedy these drawbacks, novel approaches based on incorporating an aptamer, as a cell-targeting agent, and a separate functionality, as a carrier of chemotherapeutics as well as other bioimaging agents, into a single, yet multifunctional, nanostructure have recently emerged [39,48].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have emerged as a new class of nanostructured materials with ordered crystalline structures, synthetic tunability, and intrinsic porosity, and have shown tremendous potential in a variety of applications [49,50] such as gas storage and separation [51–53], catalysis [54–58], chemical sensing [59,60], energy storage [61–64], and nanomedicine [65–69]. The mesoporous structure of MOFs allows for the exceptionally high cargo loading, and their intriguing framework architectures protects the encapsulated cargo from enzymatic degradation, and improves their thermal stability [70]. In addition, MOFs are susceptible to post-synthetic modification to increase their diversity and introduce demanding properties that are suitable for biomedical applications [71,72].

Breast cancer, as the most commonly diagnosed cancer among women in global, can be divided into three main subtypes according to the expression of three important markers; estrogen receptor (ER), progesterone receptor (PR), and human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2) [73]. However, triple-negative tumors that did not express ER, PR and HER2 account for about 15–20% of all breast cancer cases and have been considered as the most aggressive phenotype of breast cancer with a worse prognosis [74]. The lack of expression of these receptors makes it more challenging to utilize commonly used targeted cancer therapy approaches. However, studies have demonstrated that nucleolin is overexpressed in the membrane of the triple-negative breast cancer cells [75]. To the best of our knowledge, there has been just one study reporting a drug delivery system that relies on an aptamer-conjugated MOF for targeted drug delivery to MDA-MB-231 as a triple-negative human breast cancer cell line. In that study [66], an AS1411 aptamer-conjugated MOF (with amino triphenyldicarboxylic acid linker) has been used, which revealed a ca. 50% of cell death after a time interval of 5 days in this cell line. In the present study, we report an aptamer-functionalized and carbon dots (CDs)-encapsulated Fe3O4@MOF core-shell nanostructure that combines the pH-responsive targeted drug delivery and fluorescence imaging capabilities into one system (Scheme 1 ). This nanocarrier consists of a nucleolin-specific DNA aptamer conjugated to a nanostructure based on magnetite core and zirconium 2-amino-1,4-benzenedicarboxylate (known as UiO-66-NH2) MOF shell. To apply for the fluorescence imaging, we encapsulated carbon dots into the synthesized Fe3O4@MOF nanostructure, thereby endowing fluorescence features to the nanocarrier. The ability of this nanocarrier to deliver DOX and induce apoptosis in the triple-negative MDA-MB-231 human breast cancer cell line, as well as its potential for cellular imaging is investigated here. In addition, we investigated the possible toxic effect of the nanocarrier in HUVAC normal epithelial cells.

Iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), and gelatin were purchased from Merck, Germany. Zirconium chloride (ZrCl4), 2-amino-1,4-benzenedicarboxylic acid, (NH2-BDC, also known as 2-aminoterephthalic acid), poly (acrylic acid) (PAA, M.W~3,000), urea, ethylene glycol, 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), doxorubicin hydrochloride, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-[3-(dimethylamino)propyl]-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), and dialysis membranes (molecular weight cut-off, MWCO, 10 ​kDa) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals, USA. Ham’s F12, Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM), Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) and Penicillin-Streptomycin (pen-strep) were obtained from Life Technologies (Paisley, Scotland). All the other reagents were of analytical grade and were used as received without further purification. Deionized (DI) water was used for the preparation of all the solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the Fe3O4@MOF core-shell nanostructures
ce:section.ce:para: For the preparation of the Fe3O4@MOF core-shell nanostructures, 25 ​mg of Fe3O4 was added to 10 ​mL of DMF and ultrasonicated for 30 ​min (preparation of Fe3O4 is described in the Supporting Information, SI). Then, the obtained dispersion was added to 8 ​mL of DMF solution containing precursors of the UiO-66-NH2 MOF (37.5 ​mg of ZrCl4, and 29 ​mg of NH2-BDC). The resulting mixture was placed in a preheated oven at 80 ​°C for 12 ​h and then held at 100 ​°C for 24 ​h. After cooling down to room temperature, the resulting pale brown solid was magnetically separated and washed several times with ethanol and DI water. The purified Fe3O4@MOF core-shell nanostructures were dispersed in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) and stored at 4 ​°C for subsequent use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of DOX-loaded and CDs-labeled Fe3O4@MOF
ce:section.ce:para: Loading of DOX onto the Fe3O4@MOF nanocarriers was carried out by mixing an aqueous suspension of the Fe3O4@MOF nanostructures with a predefined concentration of DOX (dissolved in PBS, pH 8.0) for 24 ​h. To remove the excess unloaded DOX, we collected the magnetic nanoparticles by magnetic separation and removed the supernatant by a pipette, and washed the particles with the PBS buffer for several times. For covalent conjugation of highly fluorescent carbon dots to the Fe3O4@MOF-DOX nanocarrier, we activated the carbon dots solution by EDC (50 ​mg) followed by the addition of NHS (50 ​mg). We thoroughly mixed the reaction mixture and kept the solution for 3 ​h in dark to activate the acid groups. To this solution, we dispersed 15 ​mg of Fe3O4@MOF-DOX and the reaction was continued for 12 ​h in dark. Then, we collected the particles by a magnet and washed them carefully with DI water to remove the unbounded carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of the aptamer to Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: For specific targeting, we conjugated a nucleolin-binding aptamer (Apt., namely AS1411) to the Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs nanostructures. To accomplish this, we dissolved Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs (1.0 ​mg) in 1.0 ​mL of DI water by 30 ​min gentle ultrasonication. Then, we added aptamer (500 ​μL, 1.0 ​μM) to the aforementioned solution with stirring at room temperature for 5 ​min, and kept the mixture in dark at 4 ​°C overnight. Finally, we separated the Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt nanocarriers from the reaction mixture by centrifugation.

Normal epithelial cells (HUVEC) and human epithelial breast cancer cells (MDA-MB-231) were cultured in a 1:1 mixture of Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) and Ham’s F12 media supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% of penicillin-streptomycin (pen-strep, 100 U ​mL−1-100 ​μg ​mL−1) and incubated in a humidified 37 ​°C atmosphere containing 5% CO2 [76]. To subculture the cells, once 90% confluence is reached, HUVEC and MDA-MB-231 ​cells were incubated with trypsin-EDTA for 2–5 ​min. The cells were subsequently subdivided and reseeded into fresh cultures. Treatment of the cells with nanocarriers and the details of the MTT assay as well as the cell proliferation assay are presented in the SI file.

The physico-chemical characteristics of the as-synthesized core-shell Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt nanocarriers along with their building blocks were examined by a variety of microscopic and spectroscopic techniques including field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), selected area electron diffraction (SAED), ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis), photoluminescence (PL), Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), as well as Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) specific surface area and Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) pore-size distribution analyses.

The surface morphology, size, and mono-dispersity of the as-synthesized magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles were inspected using FE-SEM. The FE-SEM image of the nanoparticles shows that spherical particles, with a diameter of ~12 ​nm, and smooth surfaces are formed (Fig. S1a). The FE-SEM image of the MOF nanoparticles displays a highly monodisperse morphology with an average diameter of ~17 ​nm (Fig. S1b). Morphological characteristics, such as narrow size distribution, spherical shape, and mono-dispersity were retained in the case of the Fe3O4@MOF nanostructures (Fig. 1 a). The average diameter of the Fe3O4@MOF nanostructures was ~16 ​nm. Note that conventional synthesis of MOFs comprises of three major steps: (1) mixing metal ions and organic linkers in a solvent; (2) nucleation and growth of the MOF particles; and (3) purification and activation of the product. However, synthesis of MOF nanoparticles via a conventional method is a very challenging task, because of the very fast nucleation and growth processes of MOFs in a solution phase [77]. It has previously been reported that colloidal particles provide active surface sites for the controlled, in situ formation of MOF nanoparticles [78]. The exterior surface of the Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles is rich in a variety of functional groups that interact with metal ions and organic linker to promote heterogeneous nucleation and in situ growth of MOF nanoparticles. The presence of heterogeneous nucleation sites promotes the generation of more nuclei, resulting in the successful formation of tiny MOF particles. EDS spectroscopy (Fig. S2) confirmed the presence and the uniform distribution of the expected elements in the corresponding nanoparticles (see SI).

We structurally characterized the Fe3O4@MOF nanostructures using TEM that allows for a spatially resolved characterization, ideally suited for the study of multi-layered nanostructures. Fig. 1b shows the TEM micrograph of the Fe3O4@MOF nanostructures with an average size of ~16 ​nm and a uniform shape. The TEM image of the Fe3O4@MOF nanostructures shows the presence of a lower contrast part (~5.5 ​nm thick) surrounding a darker central part (Fig. 1b). The image also shows a lighter region between the individual nanoparticles. This implies that Fe3O4@MOF nanostructures are in the form of a core@shell arrangement. The SAED pattern of the Fe3O4@MOF core-shell nanostructures clearly shows many strong diffraction spots, demonstrating the well-defined polycrystalline nature of these nanoparticles (Fig. 1b, inset). The TEM images and SAED pattern of the as-synthesized carbon dots are presented in Fig. 1c. The TEM images show that the carbon dots are distributed in a narrow range around a mean value of ~9.5 ​nm, and the SAED pattern indicates that the structure of the carbon dots is amorphous (halo rings) and crystalline (diffraction spots), in part.

We also characterized surface functional groups of the magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles, MOF shells, free DOX, carbon dots, and aptamer as well as the Fe3O4@MOF-DOX, Fe3O4@MOF-CDs, Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs and Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt nanostructures with FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2 A and Fig. S3). The FT-IR spectrum of Fe3O4@MOF-CDs exhibits both the characteristic peaks of Fe3O4@MOF and carbon dots, confirming the successful attachment of carbon dots on the Fe3O4@MOF nanoparticles. The detailed interpretation of the FT-IR spectra is provided in the SI, FT-IR characterization section. We also used FT-IR spectroscopy to provide evidence for the conjugation of the AS1411 aptamer to the Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs nanostructures. As shown in Fig. 2A, the intense and broad band centered at 3440 ​cm−1 is ascribed to the O–H stretching mode of crystalline water and physisorbed water condensed inside the cavities. The bands at 2927 ​cm−1 and 2850 ​cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of the C–H bonds. The intense doublet at 1563 ​cm−1 and 1410 ​cm−1 is ascribed to the in- and out-of-phase stretching modes of the carboxylate group, and the band at 740 ​cm−1 is attributed to the Zr–O bond.

The degree of crystallinity, phase purity and physical properties of the Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles, UiO-66-NH2 MOF, carbon dots, Fe3O4@MOF, and Fe3O4@MOF-CDs were determined by XRD spectroscopy (Fig. 2B). The Fe3O4 nanoparticles yielded the characteristic XRD peaks indexed at 2θ ​= ​30.01°, 35.40°, 43.19°, 53.30°, 57.09°, 62.82° and 74.36°, which correspond to the reflection plane indices of (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), (440), and (622), respectively. The synthesized Fe3O4 nanoparticles formed a crystalline cubic structure. The plot b in Fig. 2B shows the XRD pattern of the UiO-66-NH2 MOF. All the well-defined diffraction peaks of the as-synthesized UiO-66-NH2 MOF match well with the XRD patterns that are reported in the literature [79,80], and are consistent with simulated UiO-66-NH2 crystalline structure (Fig. S4), based on the crystallographic information file from Cambridge Crystallographic Data Centre (CCDC 837796 for the similar parent structure, UiO-66). Both the bare Fe3O4 nanoparticles and the Fe3O4@MOF nanostructures display characteristic diffraction peaks that are consistent with those of magnetite, demonstrating that formation of the MOF shell does not alter the crystalline structure of the Fe3O4 particles. However, the peaks of MOF are not detected in the core@shell structure. It has been proven that the crystallinity of MOF particles depends on the amount of solvent occupying the inside pores [81–83]. Thus, it is likely that crystalline-to-amorphous transformation occurs for the MOF shell when the relatively thin (~5.5 ​nm) MOF shell is heated in an oven during the synthesis, whereas the larger (~17 ​nm) pure MOF particles preserve their crystalline structure because of conserving (at least parts of) their solvent.

The XRD spectrum of carbon dots typically shows amorphous nature between 2θ ​= ​18–24° [84,85]. A prominent broad XRD diffraction pattern at about 2θ ​= ​~23° depicts the formation of amorphous nanoscale carbon dots in a solvothermal method. Besides the corresponding characteristics peaks of Fe3O4@MOF, the diffraction peaks of amorphous carbon dots can also be observed in the XRD spectrum of the Fe3O4@MOF-CDs nanostructures. Putting all these pieces of evidence together, along with the results of FT-IR and fluorescence imaging (provided later on), reveal that carbon dots were successfully synthesized and properly conjugated to the Fe3O4@MOF particles.

We characterized the successful conjugation of the aptamer to the nanocarrier by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. The solution of aptamer exhibits a characteristic absorption band at about 260 ​nm (Fig. 2C). After conjugation of aptamers to the Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs nanostructures, the intensity of the absorption band of the aptamer in the supernatant (at about 260 ​nm) diminishes significantly (Fig. 2C), indicating that aptamers are conjugated to the Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs nanostructures. The amount of aptamer conjugated to the nanocarrier was calculated to be about 0.16 ​μg per 1.0 ​mg of the Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs nanostructures. We studied the optical properties of the carbon dots by photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. A broad emission band centered at 410 ​nm was observed in the emission spectrum when it was excited at 330 ​nm (Fig. 2D). The well dispersed aqueous suspension of carbon dots exhibits bright green emission under UV light, which could be easily observed with the naked eye and taken with a digital camera (inset of Fig. 2D). Moreover, the maximum emission wavelength moves to the longer wavelengths with increasing the excitation wavelength, and the intensity of the emission bands also decreases (Fig. 2E). As can be seen, by increasing the excitation wavelength from 330 ​nm to 430 ​nm, the emission peak shifts from 430 ​nm to 490 ​nm and its intensity decreases gradually.

The formation of MOF shells on the surfaces of the Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles was further confirmed by carrying out thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA), the details of which are provided in the SI file (Fig. S5). We also studied superparamagnetism of the Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@MOF nanostructures (see SI and Figs. S6 and S7), which is an essential characteristic in biomedical applications such as targeted drug delivery or magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). We also characterized the surface area and porosity of the pure MOF as well as the as-synthesized Fe3O4@MOF nanostructures by N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms (Fig. S8).

To evaluate the drug loading capacity of the Fe3O4@MOF nanostructure, a commonly used anticancer drug, DOX, was mixed with Fe3O4@MOF in PBS at pH 8.0 for 24 ​h. Increasing the initial concentration of DOX up until 3.6 ​mg ​mL−1 improved encapsulation efficiency (Fig. 3 A). After incubating the different amounts of DOX with Fe3O4@MOF nanostructures and removing the excess unloaded DOX, the drug loaded nanocarrier (Fe3O4@MOF-DOX) was obtained. UV–vis–NIR spectra of the DOX solution before and after incubation with Fe3O4@MOF nanostructures were recorded to evaluate the DOX loading efficiency (Fig. 3B and C). Absorbance spectrum of the free DOX molecules in water displays a maximum at 500 ​nm, accompanied with two shoulders at about 540 and 552 ​nm (Fig. 3B). The decrease in intensity of the characteristic peak of DOX at 500 ​nm after incubation with Fe3O4@MOF nanocarriers confirms the successful loading of DOX into the Fe3O4@MOF nanocarriers (Fig. 3C). Quantitative analysis of the spectra shows that 3.16 ​mg ​mL−1 of DOX is loaded out of the initial 3.60 ​mg ​mL−1 solution.

We also characterized DOX loading into the nanocarriers using FT-IR spectroscopy. The presence of the characteristic absorption bands of DOX (marked with a rectangle) in the FT-IR spectrum of the Fe3O4@MOF-DOX nanostructures indicates the incorporation of DOX molecules into the nanostructures (Fig. 3D). A remarkable diminution of UV–vis absorption of DOX (Fig. S9) along with color change from brown to wine red for the Fe3O4@MOF-DOX dispersion (Fig. S7) confirms the strong interaction between DOX and Fe3O4@MOF. Potential interactions include: π–π stacking between the aromatic anthracycline of DOX and the aromatic pore walls of the MOF, hydrogen bonding between the oxygen atoms of DOX and the amino groups of the MOF, van der Waals forces, electrostatic interactions, and coordination bonding [86–88]. Among the above-mentioned interactions, stable coordination bonding between the deprotonated hydroxyls in DOX and the numerous Zr sites in the MOF framework play the leading role in drug loading [89,90].

The in vitro cytotoxicity of Fe3O4@MOF, Fe3O4@MOF-DOX, Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs, and Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt were evaluated by applying the MTT assay to the MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells and normal HUVEC cells. Fig. 4 A shows the cell viability of HUVEC after treatment with Fe3O4@MOF, Fe3O4@MOF-DOX, Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs, and Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt nanocarriers. As expected, the HUVEC cells treated with the mentioned nanocarriers showed the high viability of at least ~90% and up to 100 ​± ​1%, indicating no toxicity of the nanostructures.

Fig. 4B shows the in vitro cell viability studies of the different concentrations of Fe3O4@MOF, Fe3O4@MOF-DOX, Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs, and Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt against MDA-MB-231 ​cells, which were evaluated by the MTT assay. The MDA-MB-231 ​cells treated with Fe3O4@MOF showed negligible toxicity (89% cell viability), even at a high concentration of up to 3,000 ​μg ​mL−1 (Fig. 4Ba). In contrast, cytotoxicity of Fe3O4@MOF-DOX, Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs, and Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt showed a dose-dependent manner against MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells. So that, by increasing the nanocarriers concentration (plots b-c in Fig. 4B) the viability of the cells decreased. More than 77% of MDA-MB-231 ​cells were killed after incubation with 3,000 ​μg ​mL−1 of the Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt, whereas the viability of these cells incubated with the same concentration of Fe3O4@MOF-DOX and Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs were 39% and 32%, respectively. The statistical analysis of the data using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with the level of significance at p ​< ​0.05, indicated a significant difference among Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt, Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs, and Fe3O4@MOF-DOX. It means that the application of the aptamer significantly increases the efficiency of the DOX delivery and death induction in the cancer cells.

To figure out the mechanism of cell death in MDA-MB-231 ​cells, Annexin V-FICT/propidium iodide (PI) staining was performed and the live, apoptotic, and necrotic cells were quantified by flow cytometry (Fig. 4C). The data generated by flow cytometry were plotted in two-dimensional dot plots that can be divided into four sub-populations corresponding to: (Q1) necrotic cells (annexin V−/PI+), (Q2) late apoptotic cells (annexin V+/PI+), (Q3) early apoptotic cells (annexin V+/PI−), and (Q4) live cells (annexin V−/PI−). As the data show, apoptosis is the major mode of death in the MDA-MB-231 ​cells after treatment with the mentioned nanocarriers. There are significant increases in the sum of the percentages of the early and late apoptosis in the cells treated with Fe3O4@MOF-DOX (31.9%), Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs (28.8%) and Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt (39.4%) in comparison with the control cells (5.27%).

These data indicate that Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt is the most effective nanocarrier for targeted drug delivery and apoptosis induction in these cancer cells. It is because of the presence of AS1411 as a nucleolin-specific DNA aptamer on the outer shell of the nanocarrier, which binds specifically to nucleolin at the surface of cancer cells and triggers the DOX release into the cell under the mildly acidic condition (pH 5.5).

It should be noted that, previous studies by Fu and co-workers [91] on the metabolism, biodistribution, and long-term toxicity of the Fe3O4@UiO-66 nanostructures after 1, 7, 14 and 30 days of injection have shown that the nanostructures are excreted from the liver but not from the kidneys. For biodistribution study, they collected the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney) of the mice after 1, 7 and 30 days of treatment with Fe3O4@UiO-66, and measured the amount of Fe and Zr using atomic adsorption spectroscopy and the inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), respectively. Their results have shown that the level of Fe constantly decreased and nearly dropped back to the normal levels after 30 days, in all measured organs except the liver, in which the Fe level was slightly higher than the normal value. Interestingly, Zr was not detected in the organs, demonstrating that Zr could be gradually metabolized over time. In addition, blood analyses of a variety of kidneys and liver function markers, at different time intervals after the injection of the Fe3O4@UiO-66 nanostructures have shown the normal levels of all biomarkers compared with those in the control group. It indicated that the Fe3O4@UiO-66 nanostructures did not induce any obvious liver or kidney damage, thus are considered as a safe theranostic agent for biomedical applications.

Besides precise control over drug release, it is also highly desirable to endow nanocarrier with a specific targeting functionality that can induce site-specific and efficient drug delivery. To gain more insights into the ability of the aptamer-conjugated nanocarriers to target and deliver the CDs and DOX into the cells, we carried out fluorescence imaging study in MDA-MB-231 ​cells (Fig. 5 ).

Highly fluorescent CDs, as biocompatible fluorescent tags, were attached to the nanocarrier and added in the incubation media. After treatment of the cells with the aptamer-conjugated nanocarriers, they were easily internalized into the cancer cells, through nucleolin receptor-mediated endocytosis. Then, the bright green fluorescence of CDs or the red fluorescent of DOX was observed in the cells (Fig. 5). Merging these figures indicate the entrance of both ligands into the cells. These observations indicate the high affinity of the aptamer-conjugated nanocarrier to the cancer cells, and confirms the fluorescence imaging capability of these nanocarriers.

The ability of Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt nanocarriers to release DOX was investigated at two different pH values; 5.5 and 7.4. The time-dependent accumulated drug release (ADR) curves showed a slow and sustained release pattern without any burst effect at both pH 5.5 and 7.4 (Fig. 6 ). The sustained release rate would generate a stable drug concentration and provide sufficient time for Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt to accumulate at the tumor site. During the first couple of hours, a distinctly rapid DOX release from Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt is detected. Later on, as shown in Fig. 6, about 47.3% of the DOX molecules were released over 4 days at pH 5.5, whereas 29.1% of DOX was released at pH 7.4 over the same period, indicating the sensitivity of the Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt nanocarriers to the tumor’s acidic environments. Note that the stability of nanocarriers in serum is critical to their use in drug delivery systems. The sustained DOX release over a period of 4 days along with retaining the anticancer activity for a prolonged period suggest that the Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt nanocarrier remains stable in the serum-containing media, and is most likely biocompatible [92]. The pH-responsive DOX release was controlled by the drug–matrix interactions under acidic conditions, similar to the cancer cell environment. DOX consists of an aglycone moiety and an amino-sugar residue attached via a glycoside bond to the 7-hydroxy group of the aglycone. The aglycone consists of a tetracyclic ring in which rings A, B, and C form a planar anthraquinone system [93]. The π−π stacking effect introduced by the planar aromatic rings of DOX increases the stability and loading capacity of the drug into the nanocarrier. These planar aromatic rings also allow intercalation of DOX into the DNA double helix. After intercalation, which is mainly between the two adjacent GC base pairs of the DNA, DOX is stabilized by the specific hydrogen-bonding with guanine residues. This interaction inhibits replication of the DNA and thus, the tumor growth [94,95]. Via intercalation, DOX can also induce histone eviction from transcriptionally active chromatin [96,97]. The antitumor mechanism of DOX is also through its function as a topoisomerase II poison [98]. Therefore, it would inhibit the unwinding of DNA supercoils, which is a necessary step for cancer cell replication and transcription, leading to double strand breaks [99,100]. Collectively, DOX inhibits cell proliferation through different mechanisms including the induction of DNA damage, cell cycle arrest at the G (2)/M phase, impairment of mitochondria, and finally induction of apoptotic cell death [101].

The strong electrostatic attraction also plays a major role in the adsorption of DOX to the nanocarrier. Noteworthy, the nanocarriers do not burst to drug release, thanks to the aptamer that serves here as both a targeting agent as well as a capping agent [66,102,103] against the undesired leaching or burst release problem of the common nanocarriers. The sustained drug release can also be attributed to the porosity of the nanocarriers. It implies that DOX molecules are encapsulated in the entire region of the core@shell nanostructures, and the thickness of the MOF shell is neither too small to result in the initial burst drug release, nor too thick; so that a large number of DOX molecules remain permanently entrapped within the nanocarriers.

In summary, for the first time, we have successfully designed a new class of hybrid magnetic Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt nanocarriers which exhibit high stability (up to 6 days in the serum-containing media), and safety for normal cells in vitro. It shows the enhanced DOX loading efficiency, cancer cell targeting ability and pH-sensitive drug release. The modification with an aptamer endows Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs with high selectivity for targeted cancer therapy. The Fe3O4@MOF-DOX-CDs-Apt nanocarrier targets nucleolin, overexpressed on the triple-negative MDA-MB-231 human breast cancer cells, and delivers the cargos into the nucleus of the cancer cells. So that, enhanced nucleolin-mediated internalization enabled selective delivery of the anticancer drug into the cells, which resulted in more than 77% cell death after 24 ​h. Whereas, it was completely safe for HUVEC, normal epithelial cells. Moreover, we have encapsulated carbon dots into the nanocarriers; thus, the system provides a unique platform not only for targeted drug delivery but also for optical imaging of the cells. Carbon dots conjugated to the nanocarriers serve as excellent fluorophores for the imaging of cancer cells. In a nutshell, our work addresses designing a multi-functional nanostructure with improved cancer cell-binding affinity, high tumor uptake, the ability for fluorescence imaging, and potential use as an MRI contrast agent. These features are essential prerequisites for the development of the next-generation aptamer-based therapeutic agents. This platform can also be further improved for the targeted delivery of a variety of therapeutic species as well as nucleic acids and proteins with high specificity and efficiency.

The Supporting Information associated with this paper contains syntheses of UiO-66-NH2 nanocrystals and carbon dots, drug loading and release, cell culture, cytotoxicity assay, and physico-chemical characterization of the materials (FE-SEM, EDS, FT-IR, UV–vis, thermal analysis, magnetization studies, and BET and BJH analyses).
